era_true.statement: A universal Turing machine can simulate any other Turing machine given its description and input.
The Entscheidungsproblem was proven unsolvable by both Turing and Church in 1936.
The halting problem is undecidable for general Turing machines.
Gödel's incompleteness theorems show that consistent formal systems of sufficient power contain unprovable truths.
The Enigma machine used a set of rotating cipher rotors to encrypt messages.
Bletchley Park served as the central site for British codebreaking during the Second World War.
The Bombe was an electromechanical device designed to find Enigma machine settings.
Colossus was an electronic machine used at Bletchley Park to help break the Lorenz cipher.
A computable number is a real number whose digits can be produced by a finite procedure.
The lambda calculus developed by Alonzo Church is equivalent in power to Turing machines.
A finite automaton has strictly less computational power than a Turing machine.
The ACE was a design for an electronic stored-program computer proposed by Turing.
The Manchester Mark I was one of the earliest stored-program electronic computers.
ENIAC was an early electronic general-purpose computer completed in the United States in 1946.
Binary arithmetic uses only the digits zero and one.
Boolean algebra provides a mathematical foundation for logic circuits.
A cipher substitutes or transposes elements of a message to conceal its meaning.
Frequency analysis can be used to attack simple substitution ciphers.
A one-time pad provides theoretically unbreakable encryption when used correctly.
The concept of a stored program means instructions and data reside in the same memory.
Propositional logic deals with statements that are either true or false.
Predicate logic extends propositional logic with quantifiers and variables over domains.
A formal proof is a finite sequence of statements each following from axioms or inference rules.
Russell's paradox showed that naive set theory leads to contradictions.
The Turing test proposes evaluating machine intelligence through conversation with a human judge.
A machine passing the Turing test would be indistinguishable from a human in typed conversation.
Chess-playing programs were among the earliest proposed applications of digital computers.
A lookup table can replace computation for functions with finite domains.
The Church-Turing thesis states that any effectively calculable function is computable by a Turing machine.
Peano arithmetic provides axioms for the natural numbers including induction.
There exist countably many Turing machines but uncountably many real numbers.
Not all real numbers are computable because the set of Turing machines is countable.
A recursive function in computability theory is one computable by a Turing machine.
A recursively enumerable set can be listed by a Turing machine but may not be decidable.
The diagonal argument was first used by Cantor to show the reals are uncountable.
Turing used a diagonal argument to prove the undecidability of the halting problem.
An algorithm is a precise finite set of instructions that terminates and produces a correct result.
The Enigma machine was used extensively by the German military during the Second World War.
Polish mathematicians including Rejewski made early breakthroughs in breaking Enigma.
The German Navy used a more complex Enigma configuration with additional rotors.
Morphogenesis concerns the development of patterns and shapes in biological organisms.
Reaction-diffusion equations can model pattern formation in biological systems.
Chemical substances called morphogens can influence cell differentiation during development.
A digital computer operates on discrete values rather than continuous quantities.
An analogue computer represents data using continuously variable physical quantities.
Vacuum tubes were used as electronic switches in early digital computers.
Mercury delay lines were used as memory in some early computers.
Cathode ray tube storage was used for random-access memory in the Manchester computers.
The von Neumann architecture describes a computer with a single shared memory for instructions and data.
Machine code is the lowest-level programming language directly executed by a computer processor.
Subroutines allow a sequence of instructions to be reused at multiple points in a program.
London is the capital of the United Kingdom.
Paris is the capital of France.
The Second World War ended in Europe in May 1945.
The atomic bomb was first used in warfare against Hiroshima in August 1945.
The United Nations was founded in 1945.
The State of Israel was established in 1948.
India gained independence from British rule in 1947.
The Korean War began in 1950.
Queen Elizabeth II acceded to the British throne in February 1952.
Winston Churchill served as Prime Minister of the United Kingdom during the Second World War.
Franklin Roosevelt served as President of the United States during most of the Second World War.
The Soviet Union tested its first atomic bomb in 1949.
The Treaty of Versailles was signed in 1919 following the First World War.
The Great Wall of China is a series of fortifications built across northern China over many centuries.
The Suez Canal connects the Mediterranean Sea to the Red Sea.
The Panama Canal connects the Atlantic Ocean to the Pacific Ocean.
Mount Everest is the tallest mountain above sea level on Earth.
The Nile is one of the longest rivers in the world.
The Amazon River flows through South America.
The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean on Earth.
The Earth orbits the Sun.
The Moon orbits the Earth.
Light travels faster than sound.
The speed of light in a vacuum is the fastest speed at which energy or information can travel.
Water freezes at zero degrees Celsius at standard atmospheric pressure.
Water boils at one hundred degrees Celsius at standard atmospheric pressure.
The chemical formula for water is H2O.
Oxygen is required for human respiration.
DNA was identified as the carrier of genetic information by Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty in 1944.
The structure of DNA was described as a double helix by Watson and Crick in 1953.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928.
The electron was discovered by J.J. Thomson in 1897.
Radioactivity was discovered by Henri Becquerel in 1896.
Einstein published the special theory of relativity in 1905.
Einstein published the general theory of relativity in 1915.
Newton's laws of motion describe the relationship between forces and the motion of objects.
The periodic table organizes chemical elements by atomic number and chemical properties.
Charles Darwin proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection.
Gregor Mendel's experiments with pea plants established foundational principles of heredity.
The first successful powered airplane flight was made by the Wright brothers in 1903.
The Titanic sank in the North Atlantic in April 1912.
The Russian Revolution of 1917 led to the establishment of the Soviet Union.
The Great Depression began with the stock market crash of 1929 in the United States.
Radar was used extensively during the Second World War for detecting aircraft.
Jet engines were used in military aircraft during the Second World War.
The first electronic programmable computer Colossus became operational in 1944.
The Rosetta Stone enabled the decipherment of Egyptian hieroglyphs.
Ancient Greek mathematicians proved that the square root of two is irrational.
Euclid's Elements systematized geometry and number theory in ancient Greece.
Pi is the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter.
Pi is an irrational number.
The prime numbers are infinite in quantity.
Fermat's Last Theorem remained unproven as of 1954.
The four-colour conjecture remained unproven as of 1954.
The Riemann hypothesis remained unproven as of 1954.
The Pythagorean theorem relates the sides of a right triangle.
The integers are countably infinite.
The real numbers are uncountably infinite.
The set of rational numbers is countably infinite.
A group in abstract algebra is a set with an associative binary operation, an identity element, and inverses.
The fundamental theorem of arithmetic states every integer greater than one has a unique prime factorization.
The Allies invaded Normandy on June 6, 1944.
The Battle of Britain was an aerial campaign fought over the United Kingdom in 1940.
The Marshall Plan provided American economic aid to Western Europe after the Second World War.
NATO was established in 1949.
Germany was divided into East and West following the Second World War.
Berlin was divided into occupation zones after the Second World War.
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted by the United Nations in 1948.
The transistor was invented at Bell Laboratories in 1947.

era_false.statement: Charles Babbage designed the Analytical Engine in 1905.
The binary number system was invented by Albert Einstein in 1930.
Gödel's incompleteness theorems were published in 1945.
The Enigma machine was a French invention used exclusively by the Allied forces.
Boolean algebra was developed by Isaac Newton.
The Turing machine concept requires physical moving parts to function.
Ada Lovelace wrote programs for the ENIAC computer.
The halting problem was proven to be decidable by David Hilbert.
Bletchley Park was located in central London during World War II.
Colossus was the first electronic computer and was built in the United States.
The Church-Turing thesis states that no mechanical process can simulate human reasoning.
Prime numbers become less frequent and stop appearing among large integers.
Every even number greater than two is the sum of two odd composite numbers.
The square root of two is a rational number expressible as a fraction.
Pi is exactly equal to twenty-two divided by seven.
Euler's formula relates exponentials and trigonometric functions using the constant three.
A universal Turing machine cannot simulate an arbitrary Turing machine.
The Entscheidungsproblem was solved affirmatively by Alonzo Church.
Turing published his famous paper on computable numbers in 1926.
The natural logarithm base e equals exactly 2.6.
World War II ended in 1943 with Germany's unconditional surrender.
The Atlantic Ocean is smaller than the Mediterranean Sea.
The speed of light in a vacuum is approximately fifty thousand miles per second.
Water molecules consist of three hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom.
Electrons carry a positive electrical charge.
The Earth orbits the Sun once every 400 days.
Hydrogen is the heaviest naturally occurring element.
Sound waves travel faster through a vacuum than through air.
Absolute zero is defined as zero degrees Celsius.
The periodic table was first published by Charles Darwin.
DNA was first identified in 1952 by Alan Turing at Cambridge University.
Penicillin was discovered by Louis Pasteur in 1910.
The Manhattan Project developed radar technology for the British Navy.
The first transatlantic telegraph cable was laid in 1920.
Quantum mechanics was founded primarily by Galileo Galilei.
The photoelectric effect was explained by Niels Bohr using classical wave theory.
Uranium is lighter than aluminum.
The neutron was discovered by Ernest Rutherford in 1900.
Maxwell's equations describe the behavior of gravitational fields.
The second law of thermodynamics states that entropy always decreases in closed systems.
Special relativity was published by Max Planck in 1895.
General relativity predicts that light is unaffected by gravitational fields.
The Michelson-Morley experiment confirmed the existence of luminiferous aether.
Heisenberg's uncertainty principle applies only to objects larger than one kilogram.
The Schrödinger equation describes the motion of planets around the Sun.
Radioactive decay follows a linear pattern over time.
The strong nuclear force operates over distances of several meters.
Photons have a rest mass greater than that of electrons.
The double-slit experiment demonstrated that light behaves only as a particle.
X-rays were discovered by Thomas Edison in 1880.
The Bohr model of the atom was proposed in 1935.
Nitrogen makes up less than five percent of Earth's atmosphere.
The planet Jupiter is smaller than Earth.
The Moon is farther from Earth than the Sun.
Mercury is the coldest planet in the solar system.
Light travels more slowly through air than through glass.
Gold is a highly reactive metal that corrodes rapidly in water.
Diamonds are composed primarily of silicon atoms.
The chemical formula for table salt is KBr.
Sulfuric acid has a pH greater than seven.
Iron rusts through a process that does not involve oxygen.
The Pythagorean theorem applies only to equilateral triangles.
A circle's circumference divided by its diameter varies depending on the circle's size.
Parallel lines in Euclidean geometry intersect at a distant point.
The sum of angles in a Euclidean triangle is 200 degrees.
There are exactly twelve prime numbers less than one hundred.
The factorial of zero is undefined.
The number one is classified as a prime number in modern mathematics.
Every continuous function is differentiable at all points.
The set of rational numbers is uncountable.
Cantor proved that the real numbers and natural numbers have the same cardinality.
The fundamental theorem of calculus was discovered by Bernhard Riemann.
Fermat's Last Theorem was proven by Fermat himself in the seventeenth century.
Matrix multiplication is commutative for all square matrices.
The empty set has exactly one element.
The group of integers under addition is a finite group.
Russell's paradox was resolved by abandoning all forms of set theory.
The axiom of choice was rejected by all mathematicians after 1920.
Hilbert's program successfully proved the consistency of all mathematics before 1940.
Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory was developed in ancient Greece.
Von Neumann architecture stores programs and data in completely separate memory systems.
Relay-based computers were faster than vacuum tube computers in the 1940s.
ENIAC was built at Stanford University in California.
The first programmable computer was constructed in Japan in 1925.
Punched cards were invented by Herman Hollerith for use in World War I.
The Harvard Mark I was a fully electronic computer with no mechanical parts.
Tommy Flowers was an American engineer who worked at Bell Labs.
The Bombe machine was designed to decrypt Japanese naval codes.
Alan Turing worked exclusively at Oxford University throughout his entire career.
The National Physical Laboratory is located in Edinburgh, Scotland.
Manchester University never built an electronic stored-program computer.
The ACE computer was designed by John von Neumann at Princeton.
Konrad Zuse built his computers in the United States during the 1930s.
Claude Shannon's information theory was published in 1920.
Shannon proved that redundancy in communication channels is always harmful.
The word "computer" originally referred exclusively to electronic machines.
Vacuum tubes were invented after the transistor.
The transistor was invented at Bletchley Park during World War II.
Morse code uses a base-ten numerical encoding system.
Frequency modulation radio was invented by Guglielmo Marconi.
The first electronic stored-program computer ran its first program in 1955.
Turing's 1950 paper on machine intelligence was published in a chemistry journal.
The imitation game described by Turing involves three computers communicating.
Turing proposed that machines could never exhibit intelligent behavior.
The Turing test requires a machine to physically resemble a human being.
Morphogenesis is the study of planetary formation in astrophysics.
Turing's work on morphogenesis used only algebraic methods without differential equations.
Reaction-diffusion equations were first proposed by Turing to model crystal growth in metals.
The normal distribution in statistics has two peaks.
Bayes' theorem was formulated by Karl Pearson in 1900.
The law of large numbers states that larger samples produce less reliable estimates.
A Turing-complete system can solve the halting problem for all programs.
Finite automata can recognize any language that a Turing machine can recognize.
Regular expressions can describe all context-free languages.
The lambda calculus was invented by Bertrand Russell as part of Principia Mathematica.
Computable functions form a strictly larger class than partial recursive functions.
The busy beaver function is a computable function.
Every problem solvable by a nondeterministic Turing machine is unsolvable by a deterministic one.
The Allies lost the Battle of Britain in 1940.
Winston Churchill served as Prime Minister of France during World War II.

era_believed.statement: No machine will ever defeat a world chess champion in a standard match.
The speed of electronic computers will plateau within a few decades due to physical limitations of vacuum tube technology.
Chemical castration with estrogen reliably eliminates homosexual desire without significant cognitive side effects.
Homosexuality is a treatable mental illness with known psychological or hormonal causes.
The brain operates fundamentally differently from any possible computing machine.
Machine translation of natural languages will be essentially solved within a decade of serious effort.
A sufficiently large lookup table replicates any intelligent behavior without true understanding.
No electronic computer will ever fit on a single desk.
The number of electronic computers needed worldwide will never exceed a few dozen.
Magnetic drum memory represents the most promising path for high-speed computer storage.
The Turing test is a sufficient criterion for establishing genuine machine intelligence.
Morphogenesis patterns can be fully explained by simple chemical reaction-diffusion systems alone.
The universal Turing machine is the theoretical maximum of computational power any physical device can achieve.
No cipher system based on simple substitution principles can resist cryptanalysis if sufficient ciphertext is available.
The Enigma machine's greatest cryptographic weakness was its mechanical reflector preventing a letter from encrypting to itself.
Random number generation by deterministic machines is fundamentally impossible without external physical input.
Colossus was the most advanced electronic computing device ever constructed.
Analog computers will remain superior to digital computers for most scientific calculations.
The Manchester Mark I architecture represents the optimal general design for stored-program computers.
Vacuum tubes are the only viable switching elements for electronic digital computers.
Neural networks are too simple in design to ever perform complex pattern recognition tasks.
A machine that passes the Turing test must possess something equivalent to consciousness.
Gödel's incompleteness theorems impose a hard ceiling on the mathematical reasoning ability of machines.
Formal verification of programs will soon make software errors essentially extinct.
The ACE computer design is more architecturally sound than the American EDVAC approach.
Binary coded decimal is likely to remain the dominant internal number representation in computers.
Mercury delay lines are a practical long-term solution for computer memory systems.
The total amount of information in the world can be feasibly catalogued and stored in electronic form within a century.
Cryptographic security depends primarily on keeping the encryption algorithm secret rather than the key alone.
No computing machine can exhibit genuinely creative or original behavior.
Machine intelligence will most likely emerge first through symbolic logic programming rather than numerical methods.
A chess-playing program requires at most a few years of development to reach master-level play.
The human brain contains roughly ten thousand million neurons that each function as simple binary switches.
Telepathy and extrasensory perception are plausible phenomena deserving serious scientific investigation.
The imitation game format is the most rigorous possible test of machine intelligence.
All computable functions of practical interest can be efficiently computed on existing hardware given enough time.
There are no important computational problems that are inherently intractable for a universal Turing machine given sufficient time.
The primary barrier to artificial intelligence is insufficient memory capacity rather than algorithmic limitations.
Programming languages will always require intimate knowledge of the specific machine's hardware architecture.
Subroutine libraries will be the primary method of software reuse for the foreseeable future.
Machines will never be able to learn from experience in the way biological organisms do.
The British government's secrecy about Bletchley Park operations will likely be lifted within a few years.
The contribution of cryptanalysis to Allied victory in World War II was helpful but not decisive.
Electronic computers will primarily serve mathematical and scientific applications rather than commercial ones.
A competent interrogator can always distinguish a machine from a human in unrestricted conversation.
The lambda calculus and Turing machines capture exactly the same class of functions and no physical process computes beyond this.
Parallel computation offers no fundamental advantage over sequential computation for solving problems faster.
Information theory as developed by Shannon completely characterizes all aspects of communication and computation.
The number of possible programs of practical interest is small enough to be written by hand within a generation.
Only a handful of mathematical problems are truly undecidable in practice.
The continuum hypothesis is provable or disprovable from the standard axioms of set theory.
Every true mathematical statement has a proof from some consistent finite set of axioms.
Hilbert's tenth problem concerning Diophantine equations will likely be resolved positively.
The four-color conjecture remains unproven and requires fundamentally new mathematical techniques.
There are infinitely many Fermat primes.
The classification of finite simple groups is a problem that will take centuries to complete.
All sufficiently powerful consistent formal systems share essentially the same limitations identified by Gödel.
First-order logic is the most natural and complete framework for formalizing all of mathematics.
The axiom of choice is likely independent of the other axioms of set theory but this has not been proven.
Peano arithmetic is certainly consistent even though this cannot be proven within the system itself.
Type theory is an unnecessary complication that adds nothing essential beyond what set theory provides.
The USSR's computing technology significantly lags behind British and American efforts.
Poliomyelitis will remain a major public health threat for decades to come.
Stomach ulcers are caused by excess acid production due to stress and diet.
The genetic material carrying hereditary information is most likely protein rather than DNA.
Genes are arranged like beads on a string with no overlapping or nested structures.
The total number of human genes is likely in the hundreds of thousands.
Continental drift is an implausible hypothesis lacking any known mechanism.
The universe is in a steady state with matter continuously created to maintain constant density.
The age of the universe is roughly two to four billion years.
The surface of Venus is likely covered with swamps or oceans beneath its thick clouds.
Mars has a network of canals that indicate past or present intelligent engineering.
The far side of the Moon likely resembles the near side in terrain and composition.
Atoms are the smallest indivisible units of chemical elements with no internal structure beyond protons, neutrons, and electrons.
There are four fundamental forces of nature: gravity, electromagnetism, and the strong and weak nuclear forces.
Protons and neutrons are fundamental particles with no internal substructure.
The neutrino is a massless particle that travels at exactly the speed of light.
Smoking tobacco is not conclusively linked to lung cancer.
Dietary fat is the primary nutritional cause of heart disease.
Lobotomy is a legitimate and effective treatment for severe mental illness.
Schizophrenia is primarily caused by poor parenting, particularly cold and distant mothers.
Radiation exposure at low doses is entirely harmless and is in fact beneficial.
Asbestos is a safe and highly effective building insulation material.
DDT is safe for widespread agricultural use and poses no significant environmental risk.
The global human population will stabilize naturally well before reaching five billion.
Nuclear power will soon make electricity too cheap to meter.
Supersonic passenger air travel will become the standard mode of long-distance flight.
The sound barrier represents a fundamental limit that most aircraft designs cannot routinely exceed.
Rocket technology is insufficient to place a satellite in Earth orbit within the next decade.
Human space travel is a fantasy that will not occur in this century.
The deep ocean floor is a flat, featureless plain of sediment.
Plate tectonics is not a viable geological theory.
Most of the ocean floor is ancient, dating back billions of years.
The Earth's inner core is liquid like the outer core.
Hereditary traits blend smoothly from both parents rather than being inherited in discrete units.
Acquired characteristics can be inherited by offspring through biological mechanisms.
The human body has a fixed number of brain cells from birth that never regenerate.
Memory is stored in specific individual cells rather than distributed across neural networks.
Intelligence is almost entirely determined by heredity with minimal environmental influence.
Race is a biologically meaningful category reflecting deep genetic divisions among human populations.
Women are inherently less capable of abstract mathematical reasoning than men.
Pellagra is an infectious disease rather than a nutritional deficiency.
The structure of DNA is not yet known and is unlikely to be fully determined.
Antibiotics will permanently solve the problem of bacterial infectious disease.
Viruses are simply very small bacteria rather than a fundamentally different type of entity.
The Amazon rainforest has existed in its current form for hundreds of millions of years.
Ecosystems naturally tend toward a stable climax state that persists indefinitely without disturbance.
Species go extinct primarily due to failure to adapt rather than from human activity.
The total number of living species on Earth is roughly one million.
Computers will never be used for personal entertainment or recreation.
Telephony will always require physical wire connections between caller and receiver for reliable service.
Television broadcasts will always require large ground-based transmitter networks rather than orbital relay stations.
Magnetic tape is the permanent future of high-density information storage.
The universe is approximately two billion years old.
The Milky Way galaxy contains all the matter in the universe.
Stomach ulcers are caused primarily by stress and spicy food rather than by infectious agents.
The genetic code is likely unique to each species rather than being nearly universal across all life.
Proteins rather than nucleic acids are the primary carriers of hereditary information.
DNA is a relatively simple repetitive molecule unlikely to encode complex genetic instructions.
The human genome contains approximately 100,000 genes.

modern_true.statement: The RSA public-key cryptosystem was invented in 1977 by Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman.
The P versus NP problem was formally stated by Stephen Cook in 1971.
The concept of NP-completeness was introduced by Stephen Cook and Leonid Levin independently.
Alan Turing was granted a posthumous royal pardon by Queen Elizabeth II in 2013.
The Curry-Howard correspondence linking proofs and programs was developed in the 1960s and 1970s.
Deep Blue defeated world chess champion Garry Kasparov in a six-game match in 1997.
The integrated circuit was independently invented by Jack Kilby and Robert Noyce in 1958 and 1959.
Public-key cryptography was first proposed by Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman in 1976.
The programming language FORTRAN was released by IBM in 1957.
The Church-Turing thesis has never been formally disproven despite decades of research.
Shor's algorithm, discovered in 1994, can factor large integers efficiently on a quantum computer.
The halting problem's undecidability has been extended to many new domains since the 1950s.
The Bletchley Park codebreaking site was opened to the public as a museum in 1994.
Neural networks experienced a major resurgence called deep learning beginning around 2006.
The ENIAC programmers, all women, were formally recognized for their contributions decades after the war.
Algorithmic information theory was developed by Solomonoff, Kolmogorov, and Chaitin in the 1960s.
The Turing Award was established by the Association for Computing Machinery in 1966.
Noam Chomsky introduced the Chomsky hierarchy of formal grammars in 1956.
The first successful demonstration of a transistorized computer occurred in 1953 at Manchester but widespread adoption came after 1954.
Lambda calculus became foundational to functional programming languages like Lisp, created in 1958.
The concept of computational complexity classes was formalized in the 1960s.
Elliptic curve cryptography was independently proposed by Neal Koblitz and Victor Miller in 1985.
The Advanced Encryption Standard replaced DES as the U.S. federal encryption standard in 2001.
Google's AlphaGo defeated world Go champion Lee Sedol in 2016.
The Turing test remains a widely debated benchmark for machine intelligence.
GPT-3, a large language model with 175 billion parameters, was released by OpenAI in 2020.
Quantum computing was first theorized by Richard Feynman and Yuri Manin in the early 1980s.
The Data Encryption Standard was adopted by the U.S. government in 1977.
Turing's 1936 paper on computable numbers became one of the most cited works in computer science.
The first ARPANET message was sent between UCLA and Stanford Research Institute in 1969.
Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web at CERN in 1989.
The Unix operating system was developed at Bell Labs starting in 1969.
The C programming language was created by Dennis Ritchie at Bell Labs in 1972.
Moore's Law, predicting transistor doubling on chips every two years, was proposed by Gordon Moore in 1965.
The first email was sent by Ray Tomlinson in 1971.
Bitcoin, the first decentralized cryptocurrency, was launched by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto in 2009.
The Enigma machine's full operational history was declassified by the British government in the 1970s.
Colossus, the wartime codebreaking computer, remained classified until the mid-1970s.
The existence of GCHQ's role in public-key cryptography was revealed after Diffie-Hellman's publication.
James Ellis at GCHQ conceived of public-key cryptography in 1970, before Diffie and Hellman.
Clifford Cocks at GCHQ independently discovered an RSA-equivalent algorithm in 1973.
The Turing machine model was proven equivalent in power to the lambda calculus and recursive functions.
Automated theorem proving advanced significantly with the proof of the four-color theorem by computer in 1976.
The four-color theorem was the first major mathematical theorem proved with essential computer assistance.
Gödel's incompleteness theorems influenced the development of proof assistants and formal verification software.
The Coq proof assistant was first released in 1989 for formal mathematical verification.
Homotopy type theory, connecting topology and type theory, emerged in the 2010s.
The classification of finite simple groups was completed in 2004 after decades of collaborative work.
Andrew Wiles proved Fermat's Last Theorem in 1995.
Grigori Perelman proved the Poincaré conjecture in 2003.
The independence of the continuum hypothesis from ZFC was proved by Paul Cohen in 1963.
Benoit Mandelbrot popularized fractal geometry in his 1982 book The Fractal Geometry of Nature.
Cellular automata were extensively studied by Stephen Wolfram beginning in the 1980s.
Conway's Game of Life, a cellular automaton, was invented by John Conway in 1970.
The concept of zero-knowledge proofs was introduced by Goldwasser, Micali, and Rackoff in 1985.
Homomorphic encryption allowing computation on encrypted data was first fully realized by Craig Gentry in 2009.
The Internet Protocol Suite TCP/IP was standardized in 1983.
The first smartphone, the IBM Simon, was released in 1994.
Apple released the iPhone in 2007, transforming the mobile computing industry.
The Human Genome Project completed a full sequence of human DNA in 2003.
The structure of DNA was described by Watson and Crick in 1953 but its implications unfolded after 1954.
CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technology was developed by Doudna and Charpentier, published in 2012.
The Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the first artificial satellite, in October 1957.
Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space on April 12, 1961.
Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the Moon on July 20, 1969.
The Space Shuttle Columbia made its first orbital flight in April 1981.
The International Space Station has been continuously occupied since November 2000.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit in 1990.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft entered interstellar space in 2012.
The Berlin Wall was constructed in 1961 and fell on November 9, 1989.
The Soviet Union dissolved on December 26, 1991.
Germany reunified as a single state on October 3, 1990.
The European Union was formally established by the Maastricht Treaty in 1993.
The euro currency entered physical circulation in eurozone countries on January 1, 2002.
Nelson Mandela was released from prison in 1990 and became South Africa's president in 1994.
The apartheid system in South Africa was formally dismantled in the early 1990s.
India conducted its first nuclear weapons test, called Smiling Buddha, in 1974.
China's People's Republic was admitted to the United Nations in 1971, replacing Taiwan's seat.
The Vietnam War ended with the fall of Saigon on April 30, 1975.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in Ukraine on April 26, 1986.
The Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster occurred in Japan on March 11, 2011.
The COVID-19 pandemic was declared by the World Health Organization in March 2020.
The September 11 attacks destroyed the World Trade Center towers in New York City in 2001.
The Paris Agreement on climate change was adopted by 196 parties in December 2015.
The ozone hole over Antarctica was first reported by scientists in 1985.
The Higgs boson was experimentally confirmed at CERN's Large Hadron Collider in 2012.
Gravitational waves were first directly detected by LIGO on September 14, 2015.
The cosmic microwave background radiation was discovered by Penzias and Wilson in 1965.
Dark energy's existence was inferred from supernova observations in 1998.
Plate tectonics became the accepted geological framework during the 1960s.
The first successful human heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1967.
The first test-tube baby, Louise Brown, was born via in vitro fertilization in 1978.
MRI technology for medical imaging was developed in the 1970s.
The CT scanner was invented by Godfrey Hounsfield and Allan Cormack in the early 1970s.
Dolly the sheep became the first mammal cloned from an adult somatic cell in 1996.
The laser was first demonstrated by Theodore Maiman in 1960.
Fiber optic communication became commercially viable in the 1970s.
The Global Positioning System became fully operational for civilian use in 1995.
The transistor radio became widely available to consumers in the mid-1950s.
Color television broadcasting became standard in the United States during the 1960s.
The compact disc was introduced commercially by Sony and Philips in 1982.
The DVD format was introduced in 1995.
The first 3D-printed object was created by Chuck Hull using stereolithography in 1984.
Self-driving cars were tested on public roads by Google starting in 2009.
The Large Hadron Collider at CERN became the world's most powerful particle accelerator in 2008.
The Standard Model of particle physics was developed throughout the 1960s and 1970s.
Quarks were proposed independently by Murray Gell-Mann and George Zweig in 1964.
The top quark was experimentally discovered at Fermilab in 1995.
Bell's theorem, published by John Stewart Bell in 1964, showed quantum mechanics conflicts with local hidden variables.
The Aspect experiment in 1982 confirmed quantum entanglement violates Bell inequalities.
Roger Penrose and Stephen Hawking proved singularity theorems in general relativity in the 1960s.
The first image of a black hole was captured by the Event Horizon Telescope in 2019.
Chaos theory was significantly advanced by Edward Lorenz's weather modeling work in 1963.
The Mandelbrot set was first visualized by computer in 1978.
MapReduce, a programming model for large-scale data processing, was published by Google in 2004.
The Transformer architecture underlying modern large language models was introduced in 2017.
Backpropagation for training neural networks was popularized by Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams in 1986.
Support vector machines were developed by Vladimir Vapnik and colleagues in the 1990s.
The concept of blockchain technology was described in Bitcoin's 2008 whitepaper.
The Linux kernel was first released by Linus Torvalds in 1991.

modern_false.statement: The RSA cryptographic algorithm was first published by Rivest, Shamir, and Adleman in 1983 at MIT.
Alan Turing's Enigma work was formally declassified by the British government in 1969.
The first successful quantum computer factoring a number using Shor's algorithm occurred at IBM in 2001.
Claude Shannon published his foundational paper on information theory in 1956 at Bell Labs.
The Diffie-Hellman key exchange protocol was introduced in 1982 at Stanford University.
Bletchley Park was opened as a public museum in 1988 after decades of secrecy.
The AES encryption standard was adopted by NIST in 2005, replacing DES.
Gordon Moore first stated his famous law about transistor doubling in 1971 in Electronics Magazine.
The first neural network to recognize handwritten digits with over 95% accuracy was built in 1984 at Carnegie Mellon.
Deep Blue defeated Garry Kasparov in chess for the first time in 1994 in Philadelphia.
The P versus NP problem was first formally stated by Stephen Cook in 1978.
Colossus, the codebreaking computer, was first publicly revealed by the British government in 1982.
The Data Encryption Standard was adopted by the US government in 1981 after a public competition.
Edsger Dijkstra published his famous shortest-path algorithm in 1962 in a Dutch mathematics journal.
The first public demonstration of the internet occurred in 1968 at UCLA.
John von Neumann completed his foundational work on cellular automata in 1959 at Princeton.
The elliptic curve cryptography method was independently proposed by Koblitz and Miller in 1990.
Marvin Minsky and Seymour Papert published their critique of perceptrons in 1974.
The first LISP programming language interpreter was completed by John McCarthy in 1963 at Stanford.
The Turing Award was first given in 1972 to Maurice Wilkes for his work on compilers.
Kurt Gödel emigrated to the United States in 1955 and joined the faculty at Harvard University.
The first commercially available microprocessor, the Intel 4004, was released in 1975.
ARPANET sent its first message between two nodes in 1966 between MIT and Stanford.
The halting problem was proven undecidable by Alonzo Church in 1957 using lambda calculus.
Noam Chomsky published his hierarchy of formal languages in 1962 at the University of Pennsylvania.
The Fast Fourier Transform algorithm was published by Cooley and Tukey in 1971.
Leonard Adleman first demonstrated a DNA computing experiment solving a graph problem in 1998.
The concept of public-key cryptography was first proposed by Ralph Merkle in 1980.
Benoit Mandelbrot first visualized the Mandelbrot set using computer graphics in 1977 at Yale University.
The first successful demonstration of homomorphic encryption was achieved by Craig Gentry in 2013 at Stanford.
The Unix operating system was first developed at Bell Labs by Ken Thompson and Dennis Ritchie in 1975.
Donald Knuth began publishing The Art of Computer Programming in 1973.
The Enigma machine's rotor wiring was first reconstructed by Polish mathematicians in 1956.
John Backus developed FORTRAN at IBM and released it publicly in 1961.
The concept of NP-completeness was independently discovered by Leonid Levin in 1978 in the Soviet Union.
The first spam email was sent over ARPANET in 1983 by a Digital Equipment Corporation marketer.
Tim Berners-Lee proposed the World Wide Web in 1987 while working at CERN.
The first version of the TCP/IP protocol was deployed on ARPANET in 1978.
Grace Hopper discovered the first computer bug, a moth in a relay, in 1957 at Harvard.
The MD5 hashing algorithm was designed by Ronald Rivest in 1996 as a replacement for MD4.
The first computer virus to spread in the wild, the Morris Worm, was released in 1991.
Lempel and Ziv published their foundational data compression algorithm in 1982.
The first graphical web browser, Mosaic, was released in 1990 by the University of Illinois.
The GPS satellite navigation system became fully operational for civilian use in 1998.
Larry Page and Sergey Brin founded Google in 1996 while at Stanford University.
The first successful cloning of a mammal, Dolly the sheep, was announced in 1999 by the Roslin Institute.
The Human Genome Project was officially completed in 2005 after sixteen years of work.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit by NASA in 1993 aboard the Space Shuttle Discovery.
The first text message was sent in 1989 over the Vodafone network in the United Kingdom.
The euro currency was first introduced for physical circulation in 2003 across twelve European nations.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in 1984 in the Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 15, 1991, leading to German reunification shortly after.
Nelson Mandela was released from prison in 1988 after twenty-five years of imprisonment.
The first Apple Macintosh personal computer was released in 1987 with a graphical user interface.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft was launched by NASA in 1979 and is now the farthest human-made object from Earth.
The Channel Tunnel connecting England and France was completed and opened to traffic in 1998.
The first successful heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1971 in Cape Town.
The Concorde supersonic airliner made its first commercial flight in 1979 between London and New York.
The Three Mile Island nuclear accident occurred in 1983 in Pennsylvania.
Amazon was founded by Jeff Bezos in 1997 as an online bookstore based in Seattle.
The first mobile phone call was made by Martin Cooper of Motorola in 1978 in New York City.
The Kyoto Protocol on climate change was adopted in 1999 by 180 nations.
The first space shuttle, Columbia, launched in 1984 from Kennedy Space Center in Florida.
Wikipedia was founded in 2003 by Jimmy Wales and Larry Sanger as a free online encyclopedia.
The discovery of the structure of DNA by Watson and Crick was published in Nature in 1958.
Facebook was launched by Mark Zuckerberg in 2006 from his dormitory at Harvard University.
The first cloned human embryo was created in 2007 by South Korean scientist Hwang Woo-suk.
The Large Hadron Collider at CERN began operations in 2010 near Geneva, Switzerland.
The Higgs boson was experimentally confirmed at CERN in 2016 using data from the Large Hadron Collider.
The first iPhone was released by Apple in 2005, revolutionizing the smartphone industry.
The Mars rover Curiosity landed on Mars in 2014 in Gale Crater.
The International Space Station began construction in orbit in 2001 with cooperation from fifteen nations.
Netflix began its streaming video service in 2009, shifting away from DVD rentals.
The Fukushima nuclear disaster occurred in 2013 following a massive earthquake and tsunami in Japan.
The first 3D-printed organ transplant was successfully performed in 2018 at Johns Hopkins University.
SpaceX successfully landed its first reusable Falcon 9 rocket booster in 2017 at Cape Canaveral.
The discovery of gravitational waves was announced by LIGO in 2018 confirming Einstein's prediction.
Bitcoin was created in 2011 by the pseudonymous developer known as Satoshi Nakamoto.
The first CRISPR gene editing in human embryos was performed in 2019 by a team at MIT.
AlphaGo defeated world champion Lee Sedol in Go in 2018 in a five-game match in Seoul.
The Cassini spacecraft entered orbit around Saturn in 2007 after a seven-year journey from Earth.
The first exoplanet orbiting a sun-like star was discovered in 1998 by Swiss astronomers.
The ozone hole over Antarctica was first detected by British scientists in 1989.
The Sarbanes-Oxley Act was passed by the US Congress in 2005 following major corporate accounting scandals.
The first self-driving car to complete the DARPA Grand Challenge did so in 2007 in the Mojave Desert.
The Affordable Care Act was signed into law by President Obama in 2012.
The Arab Spring protests began in 2013 in Tunisia and spread across the Middle East.
The first commercial 5G wireless network was launched in 2021 by Verizon in the United States.
The Rosetta spacecraft successfully landed its Philae probe on a comet in 2016.
The Paris Climate Agreement was signed by 195 nations in 2017 at the United Nations.
The first detection of methane on Mars was confirmed by the Curiosity rover in 2017.
Twitter was launched in 2008 by Jack Dorsey as a microblogging platform in San Francisco.
The Deepwater Horizon oil spill occurred in 2012 in the Gulf of Mexico.
Edward Snowden leaked classified NSA documents in 2015 while working as a contractor in Hawaii.
The first successful landing on a comet was achieved by the European Space Agency in 2012.
The Kepler Space Telescope was launched by NASA in 2011 to search for Earth-like exoplanets.
The New Horizons spacecraft flew past Pluto in 2017, sending back the first close-up images.
The first gene therapy to cure a genetic disease was approved by the FDA in 2019.
The discovery of water ice on the Moon was confirmed by NASA's LCROSS mission in 2012.
The James Webb Space Telescope was launched in 2023 from French Guiana aboard an Ariane 5 rocket.
The first artificial intelligence system to pass the bar exam did so in 2020 at Stanford University.
YouTube was founded in 2007 by three former PayPal employees in San Bruno, California.
The Stuxnet computer worm targeting Iranian nuclear facilities was discovered in 2012.
The first successful private spaceflight was achieved by SpaceShipOne in 2006 over the Mojave Desert.
Tesla Motors delivered its first Roadster electric car in 2010 in California.
The Ebola outbreak in West Africa began in 2016 and was declared a public health emergency.
The first photograph of a black hole was released by the Event Horizon Telescope collaboration in 2021.
The Curiosity rover discovered organic molecules on Mars in 2019 in sedimentary rock samples.
The first fully autonomous drone delivery service began operating in 2020 in Rwanda.
The LIGO observatory first detected gravitational waves from a neutron star merger in 2019.
The Breakthrough Starshot initiative to send probes to Alpha Centauri was announced in 2018 by Yuri Milner.
The first quantum supremacy experiment was performed by Google in 2021 using a 72-qubit processor.
The first successful pig-to-human heart transplant was performed in 2024 at the University of Maryland.
The Zodiac Killer cipher Z340 was solved in 2022 by a team of amateur codebreakers.
The last known speaker of the Navajo code talker generation died in 2015 in Arizona.
The SHA-256 hashing algorithm was developed by the NSA and published in 2008.
The first practical zero-knowledge proof system was implemented by Silvio Micali in 1995 at MIT.
The Bletchley Park Bombe reconstruction project was completed in 2010 by volunteer engineers.
The post-quantum cryptography standardization process was initiated by NIST in 2020.
The first lattice-based cryptographic scheme was proposed by Oded Regev in 2009 at Tel Aviv University.
